The layman assumes that when they purchase a videogame for example they have the right to resell, lend, or otherwise tinker with as if they bought a physical product like a car or a watch.

perform actions such as remotely destroying your devices.

paragraphs.text: Hi I think that software that you purchase with a perpetual license should remain accessible and usable to you the customer even after the original company who created it is gone. Such as creative software or pay to play online videogames. I also think that you shouldn't be able to randomly revoke this license just to get people to pay for the subscription service. Not being able to be certain of whether or not something you purchased will just disappear tomorrow especially when you have the option to invest unthinkable amounts of money into some of these pieces of software like Gachagames is quite frankly incredibly disgusting and it needs to be regulated. On top of that I think that clarification of what you are purchasing so that the customer can recognize what kind of purchase agreement is made without having to read through a massive Eula because they redifined the concept of buying and purchasing a product so that you the customer no longer get to own the software you purchased. should be required by law. Or in other words. Make it illegal to use the term buy instead of lease and to redefine words into meaning different things we already have words for in order to confuse the layperson. The layman assumes that when they purchase a videogame for example they have the right to resell, lend, or otherwise tinker with as if they bought a physical product like a car or a watch. However generally publishers have redefined the word buy into meaning lease legally so you do not actually get to own what you purchased. I also think that we should have a second look at intellectual property laws as they have become quite problematic as of late as they are a lot of what gives companies the rights to perform actions such as remotely destroying your devices. In my opinion if a company needs to rely on ip laws giving them immense leverage in the industry in order to compete they do not deserve to succeed in the first place. Naturally there is a fine line between not wanting your work stolen and being able to leverage ip laws into being able to retain ownership and control over a product after it has been sold to a customer. And the current international ip laws blur that line way too much. I think you shouldn't be able to sell physical hardware such as a car or videogame console and then not include the software in the purchasing agreement but rather have a separate agreement that leases the software to the customer. Letting the company remotely disable or otherwise tamper with the product you the customer already purchased. Sure the hardware is still technically usable after the software license has been revoked. Only problem is that in order to make further use of the hardware it requires specialized knowledge in low level software development and reverse engineering skills in order to make the hardware functional again after the software has been remotely broken. I personally think that tech giants and corporations who have CEO's with salaries thousands of times higher than their own employees do not need this extra unfair leverage in order to stay afloat and avoid bankruptcy There are certainly a lot of products such as videogames that would no longer be possible to make due to changes like this such as live service games. However I think that the world would be a much better place if these sorts of practices were illegal and the top 0.02% of the population would have way less tools to hoard 90% of global wealth and hopefully should lead to more economic value circulating and leave the world in an overall better spot. (These statistics are entirely hyperbolae and are not at all accurately researched)
